title: Shocked by Bank Funding Shocks: Evidence from Consumer Credit Cards
abstract: Using the near universe of U.S. consumer credit cards, we show that banks transmit their wholesale funding shocks to consumers by reducing their credit card limits. Credit-constrained consumers who are unable to hedge against the transmitted shock by accessing other credit cards experience a stronger and more persistent reduction in aggregate credit card limits at the consumer level. Consequently, these credit-constrained consumers reduce their aggregate credit card borrowing. Our results document a credit card lending channel for the transmission of adverse bank shocks and show who bears the costs of fragile bank funding structures.

article.1 Short-Term Wholesale Funding Shock:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Credit cards are ubiquitous and serve as an important source of liquidity buffers and marginal financing for households for the consumption of goods and services (e.g., food, transportation, and healthcare), and handling emergencies and unplanned expenses.1For instance, 73% of U.S. households have at least one credit card, and about one-third of U.S. households do not pay their balances in full every month, thus effectively borrowing on credit cards.2Consequently, it is important to understand the factors that affect household borrowing on credit cards.

Using the near universe of credit cards from Equifax, a major credit bureau, we document a novel channel – namely, credit card limits – through which banks transmit their adverse funding liquidity shocks to consumers and affect their borrowing. Importantly, we show that bank funding shocks are transmitted unequally across consumers and have long-lasting effects. Thus, our study sheds light on bothwhobears the costs of fragile bank funding structures andhow persistentthese costs are.

We use the dry-up of the short-term wholesale funding market in 2008 as an unanticipated funding liquidity shock to banks. Uninsured short-term wholesale liabilities, such as repos and commercial paper, are an important source of funding for many banks. These funding markets dried up suddenly in 2008, causing negative shocks to bank liquidity (Gorton and Metrick 2012;Tarullo 2014). Wholesale funding is a significant source of funding for many banks in our sample. These banks also varied substantially in their dependence on short-term wholesale funding, thus generating plausibly exogenous variation in banks’ exposure to the wholesale funding liquidity shock.

However, it is unclear whether wholesale funding liquidity shocks to banks affect their consumers through the credit card lending channel. If banks can easily substitute their wholesale funding with another funding source on similar terms, then they may not need to pass on their funding shocks to their consumers. Similarly, while credit cards are an important source of marginal financing for many households, if consumers can switch costlessly to other credit cards, or if they have sufficient unused credit,3then the transmission of bank liquidity shocks through credit cards should not affect their credit card borrowing. Thus, frictions that constrain both banks and their consumers in the credit market are necessary for the wholesale funding shock to have a persistent impact on consumers through credit cards.

The primary challenge in identifying the transmission of the short-term wholesale funding shock to consumers through credit cards is to isolate changes in credit supply from changes in credit demand. For example, individual-specific demand factors such as income changes can affect a bank’s credit extension to an individual. So, we first focus on individuals with credit cards from multiple banks and use an individual fixed effects methodology similar toKhwaja and Mian (2008). Essentially, we compare how the credit limits on credit cards issued to thesameindividual change as a function of the issuing bank’s exposure to the liquidity shock.

We find that a bank with a one-standard-deviation greater dependence on wholesale funding in the preshock period reduces its credit limits by $1,558 more in the post-shock period when lending to the same individual. We also show the robustness of this baseline result to potential scenarios under which the individual fixed effects may not absorb all confounding credit demand factors (e.g., bank-specific credit demand due to endogenous bank-borrower matching, mismeasurement of credit demand). Similarly, we also show that our baseline results are unlikely to be driven by other potentially contemporaneous bank-level credit supply changes, or by the bank type (e.g., large banks, risky banks) or by any one particular bank. Finally, our baseline results are also robust when we try to account for potential bias due to unobservables using the methodology proposed inOster (2019).

Next, building on our baseline results, we document that the pass-through of the wholesale funding liquidity shock has real consequences for aggregate credit card borrowing. By includingallthe credit cards issued to each consumer in our analysis, we can examine whether consumers can hedge against the transmitted funding shock by substituting to other credit cards. We first show that consumers who have greater exposure to the funding shock through their banks experience a larger reduction in their total credit card limits. Next, in response, we find that such consumers reduce their total credit card balances, suggesting a reduction in credit card borrowing, because of the funding shock.

We find heterogeneous effects of the wholesale funding shock on consumers. At the card level, the pass-through of the funding shock monotonically increases with the credit constraints of the consumer, which are measured using credit utilization and credit score bins. For instance, the low–credit score (< 680) and very high–utilization ratio (⁠>90%⁠) consumers experience a 22.6% and 28.7% reduction in their credit limits relative to their preshock limits, respectively. In contrast, the prime (credit score≥680⁠) and low–utilization ratio (⁠≤50%⁠) borrowers experience a credit limit reduction of 15.2% and 15.4%, respectively.

We find similar patterns at the individual level when we aggregate the credit limits of consumers across all their credit cards. That is, the pass-through of the funding shock reduces the aggregate borrowing capacities (i.e., total credit card limits) of credit-constrained consumers by a greater extent. More importantly, this reduction in aggregate borrowing capacities is associated with a reduction in aggregate credit card borrowing after accounting for balance-shifting across cards. Heterogeneity analysis shows that the reduction in credit card borrowing is mainly driven by consumers with credit utilization ratios greater than 60% and credit scores less than 660.

Next, we study the persistence of the transmitted funding shock’s effect on consumers’ credit limits and credit card borrowing. First, we examine the total supply of credit limits by the banks affected by the funding shock. We find that it takes nearly 10 years for the supply of credit limits by these funding shock–affected banks to return to preshock levels. In contrast, the funding shock’s effect on consumers’ credit limits remains persistent over the long run since consumers are only partially (⁠≈40%⁠) able to offset the limit reduction on their affected cards by obtaining new cards or higher limits from the unaffected banks. Moreover, the extension of lower long-run limits also resulted in lower long-run credit card borrowing for affected consumers, especially in the high–utilization ratio and low–credit score segments. For example, nonprime borrowers with a one-standard-deviation greater exposure to the funding shock had $965 lower credit card borrowing even in the long run. These results underscore the long-term effects of a bank’s fragile funding structure and how these effects vary across different types of consumers.

To explore the potential channels underlying our results, we examine the funding shock’s effect on the long-run credit card debt dynamics across consumers facing different intensities of credit constraints at the time of the shock. These debt dynamics lend support to precautionary saving motives driving our results, which suggest that consumers have incentives to accumulate liquidity buffers (e.g., liquid savings, unused credit limits) as insurance to guard against adverse income fluctuations (Deaton 1991;Carroll 1997). Intuitively, these models suggest that a reduction in credit limits – that is, a reduction in a consumer’s liquidity buffer – should strengthen the consumer’s precautionary saving motive and reduce credit card borrowing to incentivize the accumulation of liquidity buffers, even for consumers who are far away from their limit. Consistent with these predictions, first, the long-run dynamics show a reduction in credit card borrowing for high-exposure consumers even when they are far from their credit limits. Second, consumers closer to their limits dramatically reduce their credit utilization ratios and build credit buffers. However, since the high-exposure individuals experience credit limit cuts due to the transmitted funding shock, they are compelled to build credit buffers by deleveraging and reducing their credit card borrowing when they are closer to their limits.

Finally, while it is challenging to pin down the exact channel, we conduct further analysis to understand what drives the persistence of the transmitted funding shock. We do not find any evidence suggesting that long-run consumer credit scores and incomes are adversely affected by consumers’ exposure to the funding shock. These results suggest that the persistence of the transmitted shock may not be explained by an adverse knock-on effects channel, in which the funding shock itself weakens a consumer’s long-run credit fundamentals and worsens credit constraints. Similarly, the results also do not indicate that the funding shock pass-through adversely affects consumers’ permanent incomes and reduces their credit demand. Instead, we find evidence suggesting that the long-run impact of the transmitted funding shock is driven by a path dependence in credit limits, where current credit limits are influenced by historical credit limits even after controlling for contemporaneous consumer credit fundamentals.

The key contribution of our paper is documenting a novel credit card lending channel through which wholesale funding shocks of banks are transmitted to households. Our credit card lending channel, using the near universe of credit card data, complements studies on the transmission of negative bank funding shocks to households through consumer lending, such as mortgages, auto loans, and personal loans (Puri, Rocholl, and Steffen 2011;Ramcharan, Verani, and Van den Heuvel 2016;Benmelech, Meisenzahl, and Ramcharan 2017;Jensen and Johannesen 2017).4However, the credit card channel differs from these alternative transmission channels in some economically important ways.

First, credit cards are more ubiquitous, and credit limits serve as an important source of liquidity buffers for households thereby affecting their intertemporal borrowing and consumption decisions. In this context, our results provide evidence consistent with theoretical models on precautionary saving motives by showing how a reduction in consumer credit limits leads to lower credit card borrowing even for those consumers whose credit constraints are not immediately binding. These results are consistent with those ofAydin (2022), who uses a field experiment to generate exogenous increases in credit limits and examines their effect on credit card borrowing, among various other outcomes.

Second, our study highlights the credit market frictions that are associated with cross-card substitution even in the long run which leads to the persistent effect of the transmitted shock. Specifically, our results highlight the heterogeneity in the ability of consumers to hedge against the transmitted funding shock by accessing credit limits from unaffected banks and by shifting borrowing across unaffected cards over the long run.

Third, our microdata on both credit limits and credit balances allow us to study both banks’ actions and consumers’ responses separately on the same credit line. This allows us to quantify the effect of funding shocks on both banks’ willingness to lend and consumers’ willingness to borrow. In this context, our study complementsAgarwal et al. (2017). While our study focuses on how banks transmit adverse funding shocks to theirexistingcredit card customers,Agarwal et al. (2017)study how banks pass through credit expansions due to monetary policy easing to new customers in the credit card market. The transmission of bank shocks can be different for new and existing customers to the extent that information frictions bind differently across these customer groups.

Finally, our results also contribute to the understanding of how adverse shocks to bank wholesale funding affect household credit. In particular, the increasing reliance of banks on this fragile funding source relative to core deposits has been the focus of post-crisis regulatory reform (Tarullo 2014). In response, the Federal Reserve has proposed to tie risk-based capital surcharges to a bank’s reliance on wholesale funding. While our study cannot speak to the overall costs and benefits of wholesale funding, our results document one potential cost of banks’ dependence on wholesale funding on the availability of unsecured financing through credit cards to households. Specifically, our results on the heterogeneity of the pass-through of the funding shock and its long-run impact shed light onwhobears the costs of fragile bank funding structures andhow persistentthese costs are.
A bank’s funding sources can be broadly divided intocore depositsandwholesale funding. While core deposits are the major source of bank funding, banks have been increasingly relying on wholesale funding, especially short-term wholesale funding, as a supplemental funding source (Amel and Hannan 1999;Choi and Choi 2021). Wholesale funding is defined using regulatory documents on banks’ liquidity and fund management and the financial stability report.5Wholesale funding includes noncore deposits (e.g., foreign deposits, brokered deposits, large time deposits), federal funds purchased and repo borrowing, and other borrowed money. This type of funding is, for instance, provided by institutional investors, such as money market funds (MMFs) and other banks.

Short-term wholesale funding consists of all wholesale funding liabilities that have a maturity of less than one year. Unlike core deposits, which are generally considered stable, short-term wholesale funding is prone to runs and liquidity shocks if the lenders that provide this type of financing choose not to roll over their funding (Gorton and Metrick 2012,Acharya, Schnabl, and Suarez 2013).Figure 1shows that short-term wholesale funding fell dramatically after 2008. This evidence is consistent with the sudden dry-up of the wholesale funding market which has been documented previously (Gorton and Metrick 2012;Tarullo 2014).6

The dry-up of the wholesale funding market may have a differential impact across banks as banks vary in their dependence on the wholesale funding market. We measure a bank’s exposure to the short-term wholesale funding shock as the ratio of the bank’s short-term wholesale funding to its total liabilities. Banks with a higher value of this measure pre-2008 are expected to be more exposed to the short-term wholesale funding liquidity shock due to the sudden dry-up of this funding market in 2008.

Figure IA.2 of theInternet Appendixprovides some evidence for the necessary conditions for our empirical tests. First, the figure shows that banks with a greater dependence on short-term wholesale funding indeed experienced a greater reduction in short-term wholesale funding in the post-shock period. Next, the figure also plots changes in the total wholesale funding, total liabilities, and equity capital with respect to the short-term wholesale funding exposure measure. These figures indicate that banks could not make up for the funding gap using other sources of funding after the loss of short-term wholesale funding.7
article.2 Identification Challenges and Empirica:
This section discusses the challenges to identify the transmission of the short-term wholesale funding shock to consumers through credit cards. There are two main identification challenges in our setting. The first challenge is to isolate credit supply changes (because of the short-term wholesale funding shock) from credit demand changes. The second identification challenge is to isolate credit supply changes due to the short-term wholesale funding shock independent of other potentially contemporaneous bank-level credit supply changes.

We use an unanticipated funding shock to banks resulting from the dry-up of the short-term wholesale funding market in 2008. Banks varied substantially in their dependence on short-term wholesale funding. Thus, banks that depended more on short-term wholesale funding arguably experienced greater unanticipated funding liquidity shocks. In a similar spirit to a difference-in-differences setup, our empirical strategy asks whether the differential exposure of banks to the economy-wide short-term wholesale funding shock leads to differential changes in our outcome variables (e.g., credit card limits).

We implement the specification inEquation (1), which is intuitively analogous to the difference-in-differences specification expressed in first differences as opposed to levels. Similar specifications also have been employed in prior studies (e.g.,Schnabl 2012;Chernenko and Sunderam 2014), and Section C of theInternet Appendixprovides a simple model to motivate our specification. We implement the following specification:

The main coefficient of interest,β, is identified using within-individual variation due to the inclusion of the individual fixed effects,ηi(Khwaja and Mian 2008). That is, holding the individual fixed,Equation (1)tests whether banks with a higher exposure to the short-term wholesale funding shock change credit limits differently than banks with a lower exposure to the shock. Thus, both observable and unobservable individual-specific characteristics and theirchangesbetween the pre- and the post-periods (e.g., changes in income, creditworthiness) will be absorbed by individual fixed-effects and will be unlikely to confound our results.

Although the fixed-effects specification inEquation (1)has been widely employed in the banking literature (seeKhwaja and Mian 2008;Schnabl 2012;Jiménez et al. 2012;Chernenko and Sunderam 2014;Jiménez et al. 2014;Chakraborty, Goldstein, and MacKinlay 2020), it relies on the assumption of symmetric (i.e., same) credit demand by an individual across the individual’s multiple bank lenders. This assumption can be violated if an individual’s credit demand is bank-specific and shocks to this credit demand are correlated with the bank’s wholesale funding liquidity shock. For instance, suppose that banks with a greater reliance on wholesale funding specialize in offering credit cards to homeowners. In this case, given the adverse shock to homeowner balance sheets after the 2008 housing market crash (Mian, Rao, and Sufi 2013), homeowners can reduce their credit demand relatively more from the wholesale-funding-dependent banks even in the absence of the wholesale funding shock. We test for such concerns by reestimating our baseline model separately for homeowners and nonhomeowners to ensure that our results are not primarily driven by the sample of homeowners.

Similarly, if individuals prefer to use only some credit cards over others, then the true measure of credit demand will be likely reflected only through their activelyused cards. Moreover, if banks reduce the limits on the infrequently used credit cards, then our results should matter less economically. Robustness checks show that our results are similar or stronger when considering only actively used credit cards, thereby mitigating the concern of infrequently-used credit cards driving our results. We also include credit-card-level variables (e.g., the age of the credit card account) and individual–bank variables (e.g., the total number of credit-related accounts between an individual-bank pair) inf(Xi,c,b)to control for differences in the information and relationship between a bank and an individual. Arguably, these variables control for the credit demanded from a bank by an individual and the credit supplied by the bank to the individual more generally.

Another potential concern is that theExposuremeasure affects our outcomes through other channels rather than through the dependence on wholesale funding. We try to mitigate this concern in two ways. First, we look at the correlates of theExposuremeasure. If these correlates suggest other channels through which theExposuremeasure affects our outcomes in the post-2008 period, then our identifying assumption will be threatened. Second, we also test whether the parallel-trends assumption holds in the preshock period to test whether exposure to the wholesale funding shock caused the change in our outcome variables, or whether there were preexisting trends.

In terms of correlates, we first include various bank-level characteristics in our vector of control variablesf(Xi,c,b)that are correlated with theExposuremeasure (e.g., bank size). This allows for these bank-level characteristics to have differential effects on credit card limits in the post-period relative to the pre-period. Effectively, this allows for a horse race between our proposed mechanism based on the wholesale funding shock versus shocks associated with the other bank-level characteristics along the lines ofJiménez et al. (2014)andCarpinelli and Crosignani (2021). For instance, including bank size controls for the possibility that the credit extended by larger banks might be lower in the post-shock period because of the greater regulatory costs imposed on large banks in the post-2008 period.

Next, in more stringent tests, we also control for bank characteristics nonparametrically using indicator variables (e.g., above/below median) and interacting these indicator variables with individual fixed effects. As a result, our coefficient of interestβis identified holding both the individual and bank-type fixed, thereby controlling more flexibly for any differences in credit supplied by and the credit demanded from different types of banks. Finally, to better understand the degree of estimation bias in our setting, we implement a methodology proposed inOster (2019). The methodology investigates the magnitude of potential estimation bias caused by unobservables by comparing the stability of the coefficient of interest across models that exclude and include controls.

An alternate method to conduct our analysis is by using the time-series panel data and includingIndividual×Time-periodfixed effects. However, this is more challenging given our data size, as it entails a larger number of fixed effects. Further, collapsing the time-series and estimating cross-sectional regressions mitigates the econometric issues related to the underestimation of standard errors in panel data that have short time dimensions (Bertrand, Duflo, and Mullainathan 2004;Angrist and Pischke 2009). Thus, the estimation procedure usingEquation (1)provides conservative standard errors.
article.3 Data:
3.1 Data construction

We use credit card data from Equifax, which is one of the three major credit bureaus in the United States8We observe information on end-of-month credit limits and credit balances at the credit card account level at a semiannual frequency. Credit balances include both interest-bearing debt carried across pay periods and spending that is paid off. Importantly, we observe these data on credit limits and balances forallthe credit card accounts associated with an individual. We also have detailed data on individuals’ credit reports, such as credit scores or utilization ratios.

Our data analysis consists of two main samples. The first sample is used for a credit-card-level analysis, which attempts to capture within-consumer variation in the wholesale funding shock across credit-card-issuing banks. As a result, our first sample is restricted to credit-card-issuing banks with a nonzero dependence on wholesale funding. The second sample is used to analyze whether consumers can substitute to other cards issued by unaffected or less-affected banks to deal with the transmitted funding shock. Thus, the second sample utilizes the near universe of credit cards, which also includes credit cards issued by banks that do not depend on wholesale funding.

For the first sample, we identify the 25 bank holding companies (BHCs) from the credit bureau’s data files that contain credit card issuers and also have a nonzero dependence on wholesale funding.9These 25 BHCs account for 75% of the market in terms of open credit cards. From this set of 25 credit card issuers, we omit six issuers that differ from the rest of the sample of banks, and we omit one issuer because of insufficient data coverage during our sample period. Of the six issuers we omit, four are foreign credit card issuers, one issuer specializes in retail store credit cards, and one issuer targets a particular segment of the U.S. population. By omitting these six issuers, we mitigate potential credit-card-specific demand factors that can confound our analysis. Our final data set consists of 18 credit card issuers that cover 7 of the top-10 credit card issuers and account for 65% of all open credit cards in the market. We also gather the quarterly BHC Y-9C regulatory filings data for these banks at the bank holding company level during the sample period.10

Next, we identify all individuals in the United States who have active credit card accounts as of January 2008. For our short-horizon baseline analysis, we obtain information on these consumers’ credit card limits and balances at the credit card account level for six semiannual archives, namely, January 2007, July 2007, January 2008 (i.e., the preshock period), January 2009, July 2009, and January 2010 (i.e., the post-shock period). Subsequently, we extend our sample period through to the January 2018 archives for our long-run analysis. Each archive is an end-of-month snapshot of the credit limit and the credit balance on each credit card in our sample.

Figure IA.4 of theInternet Appendixplots the credit card limits and balances aggregated over all the credit cards in our sample during the pre- and post-shock periods. The figure shows that both credit card limits and balances declined between 2008 and 2010. The figure also appears to suggest that the drop in credit card limits slightly preceded the drop in credit card balances.

Table 1, panel A, shows that the mean exposure to the short-term wholesale funding liquidity shock is significantly greater for the high-exposure banks compared to the low-exposure banks. In fact, Table IA.2 in theInternet Appendixshows that the high-exposure banks have a greater dependence on most of the components of wholesale funding compared to the low-exposure banks. High-exposure banks are also significantly larger than low-exposure banks. This is consistent withChoi and Choi (2021), who argue that large banks (as opposed to small banks) are better able to access wholesale funding as they face lower financing frictions. Overall, high-exposure and low-exposure banks have similar performance in terms of return-on-equity (ROE).

Table 1, panel B, presents bank-level summary statistics using the attributes of their credit card borrowers. Panel B shows that the high-exposure banks lend to borrowers with slightly higher credit scores on average. While borrowers of the high- and low-exposure banks have a similar number of credit card accounts, the borrowers of the high-exposure banks have higher credit card limits (by $4,891) and balances (by $910), but a lower utilization ratio (by 5.99 percentage points) than the low-exposure banks.

Overall, panel A shows that size is the most significant difference between the high- and low-exposure banks, while panel B suggests that the credit card borrowers of high-exposure banks are of similar credit quality compared to the borrowers of low-exposure banks. We control for these differences in banks’ balance sheets and lending characteristics in our regression analysis.
article.4 Credit-Card-Level Results:
A funding shock increases a bank’s marginal cost of extending credit because it increases the bank’s funding costs. Banks can pass on their higher funding costs to consumers either through prices (e.g., increasing interest rates on credit card borrowing) or through quantities (e.g., reducing credit limits). However, we focus on credit limits as our card-level data do not contain information on credit card interest rates. Nevertheless, in Section 5.3, we will also attempt to test whether the wholesale funding shock is transmitted through interest rates using data from RateWatch, which provides credit card interest rate data at the bank branch level.

We begin by conducting our empirical analysis at the credit card level because it allows us to better control for confounding changes in borrower quality using individual fixed effects and study how affected banks cut consumers’ credit limits. Subsequently, in Section 5, we will use the individual-level analysis to test whether individuals can hedge against the credit limit cuts by requesting higher credit limits or opening new credit cards with the unaffected banks.

4.1 Baseline results

Table 2presents the baseline results by estimating variants of our empirical specification inEquation (1). The regression sample consists of 289 million credit card accounts belonging to 84 million consumers after dropping singleton observations (i.e., individuals with only one credit card) as they do not contribute to the identification ofβ, but can bias standard errors downward.11We also standardize the exposure variable for the ease of interpretation. 4.2 Examining potential confounders

This section tests the robustness of our baseline card-level results by considering plausible scenarios under which the identification assumptions for our fixed-effects specification may be violated. We first consider potential scenarios in which the fixed effects may not absorb all confounding credit demand factors potentially because of endogenous bank-borrower matching. Next, we consider other potential contemporaneous bank-level credit supply changes that could confound our results. Further, to better understand the degree of estimation bias in our setting, we also implement a methodology proposed inOster (2019). Finally, we also test the robustness of our results using alternative measures of banks’ exposure to the short-term wholesale funding liquidity shock.

4.2.1 Are the results confounded by individual-bank-specific factors?

As mentioned before, the fixed effects specification relies on the symmetric credit demand assumption to absorb all levels and changes in confounding credit demand factors. This assumption can potentially be violated if a consumer’s credit demand is bank-specific and shocks to their credit demand are correlated with their bank’s wholesale funding liquidity shock. In this regard, the 2008 housing crisis, which adversely affected homeowners’ balance sheets (Mian, Rao, and Sufi 2013), is potentially an important confounder. For instance, if banks with a greater reliance on wholesale funding specialize in offering credit cards to homeowners, then the reduction in credit to homeowners from the wholesale-funding-dependent banks can occur even in the absence of the wholesale funding shock. We test for this concern in columns 1 and 2 ofTable 3by reestimating our baseline model separately for homeowners and nonhomeowners. The point estimate for nonhomeowners (⁠−1,389⁠) is similar to our baseline estimate, suggesting that our results are not primarily driven by the sample of homeowners.

Similarly, if consumers prefer to use certain credit cards over others, then the true measure of consumer demand will be reflected only through those frequently used credit cards. Moreover, if banks tend to reduce credit limits for infrequently used credit cards, then our results should matter less economically. We address this concern in multiple ways. First, in column 3 ofTable 3, we reestimate our baseline specification only using the actively used credit cards in our sample and find a stronger effect (⁠−2,054⁠) compared to our baseline results. Actively used cards are those with a nonzero balance in the preshock period. Next, in columns 4 and 5, we restrict our sample to individuals who have similar levels (within 10%) of credit utilization and credit balances on the multiple credit cards issued to them.13Arguably, the symmetric credit demand assumption is more likely to be satisfied in these subsamples of credit cards with relatively homogeneous credit utilization and credit balances. While these restrictions reduce our sample size considerably, we still continue to find statistically significant and economically meaningful point estimates for theExposuremeasure.

More generally, to help control for individual-bank-specific credit demand and credit supply effects, in all our card-level analyses, we also control for credit-card-level utilization, credit card age, and the total number of credit-related accounts (of all types of credit) for a consumer-bank pair. Arguably, these variables proxy for card-level credit demand, and the information and relationship between a bank and an individual, thereby helping mitigate some confounding effects due to individual-bank factors.

4.2.2 Are the results driven by other contemporaneous bank-level credit supply changes?

We mitigate concerns of other contemporaneous credit supply changes by first looking at variables that may be correlated with theExposuremeasure and testing whetherExposuremight affect our outcomes through these other channels. For instance, sinceExposureand bank size are positively correlated, it is possible that our results are capturing the credit contraction associated with the greater regulatory costs that were imposed on larger banks in the post-2008 period. Even though our baseline specification allows all our bank-level controls to have differential effects on our outcome variables in the post-period relative to the pre-period, we employ a more stringent test to mitigate such concerns.

We classify the banks in our sample into large (above-median) and small (below-median) size groups based on their pre-period assets and include the interactions of the size indicator variable with theIndividualfixed effects in our regression specification (i.e.,Individual​×​SizeGroupfixed effects). Thus, the coefficient forExposureis identified holding both the individual and the bank-type fixed. If shocks associated with bank size confound our results, then theIndividual​×​SizeGroupfixed effects should subsume most of the variation in theExposurevariable.

Table 4, column 1 shows that our point estimates are practically unchanged even after including theIndividual​×​SizeGroupfixed effects. We estimate similar interacted fixed-effects specifications in columns 2–7 ofTable 4. We find that differences in banks’ exposure to capital costs, the securitization market, and subprime borrowers are unlikely to drive our results. Further, we find that differences in banks’ crisis expectations (proxied for by banks’ pre-period loan loss reserves), and borrower information (proxied for by the total number of credit-related accounts, across all types of credit, for a consumer-bank pair) are also unlikely to drive our results.

Finally, to better understand the degree of bias in our setting, we implement a methodology proposed inOster (2019). This methodology investigates the magnitude of potential estimation bias caused by unobservables by analyzing the stability of the coefficient of interest across specifications that include and exclude control variables. Using this methodology and accounting for potential bias due to unobservables makes our baseline result stronger, with a point estimate of−1,870⁠. Overall, this exercise also provides more confidence in our point estimates and suggests that our results are less likely to be driven by confounding factors. We provide more details on the implementation ofOster (2019)’s method in Section D of theInternet Appendix.

4.2.3 Alternative exposure measures

In Table IA.4, panel A of theInternet Appendix, we show that our baseline results are robust to using alternative measures of banks’ exposure to the short-term wholesale funding liquidity shock. When constructing theExposuremeasure, columns 1 and 2 show that our results are similar when we scale by assets and stable deposits, respectively, instead of liabilities. While we use the most prevalent definition for short-term wholesale funding, in some cases this funding is also defined to include trading liabilities (i.e., trading liabilities that exclude revaluation losses on derivatives). When included, these trading liabilities only account for 4.6% of short-term wholesale funding. Nevertheless, column 3 of Table IA.4 shows that our results are robust to including trading liabilities in ourExposuremeasure.

We also consider an alternative measure of banks’ exposure to liquidity shocks based on the idea of the Basel Accord’s liquidity coverage ratio (LCR). The LCR is complementary to ourExposuremeasure because it accounts for a bank’s reliance on short-term wholesale funding. The LCR assesses the amount of high-quality assets (e.g., cash) on the assets side and the amount of fragile funding (e.g., short-term wholesale funding) and off-balance-sheet exposures on the liabilities side. Banks can deal with periods of market-wide stress better if they have more high-quality assets and less fragile funding (i.e., a higher LCR). The LCR is computed based on a weighted sum of the bank’s assets as the numerator with a greater weight given to higher-quality assets, and a weighted sum for the liabilities and off-balance-sheet items in the denominator, with a greater weight for more runnable funding. We followChoi and Choi (2021)to construct the LCR using publicly available bank data.14

Importantly, wholesale funding components have greater weights (e.g., repo borrowing has a weight of 1) and core deposits receive a weight of 0.1. As bank funding comprises only wholesale funding and core deposits, the weighted sum of the liabilities items has a high correlation of 0.84 with ourExposuremeasure. In Table IA.4, panel B, of theInternet Appendix, we estimate regressions using the LCR ratio and its numerator and denominator separately. We find that the exposure on the liabilities side had a greater impact than the exposure on the assets side in the post-shock period. Further, columns 3 and 4 show that when ourExposuremeasure is included together with the LCR ratio and its components in the same specification, the effect of LCR is completely subsumed by theExposuremeasure. Overall, these results suggest that exposure to the short-term wholesale funding shock played a significant role in the liquidity crunch experienced by banks in 2008.

4.2.4 Other robustness

We find that our results hold when we analyze theintensivemargin (i.e., credit limit changes for cards that are open in both the pre- and post-shock periods) andextensivemargin (i.e., card closures) separately. These findings are reported in columns 1–4 of Table IA.5 of theInternet Appendix.

Since we cluster standard errors at the bank level in our analysis, the small number of clusters could potentially bias our standard errors. To mitigate this concern, we rerun our baseline analysis using the wild cluster bootstrap method as described inCameron, Gelbach, and Miller (2008). As this method requires multiple resamplings of our large data set, it is computationally intensive and practically unmanageable given our computational resource capacity. Thus, we use the wild cluster bootstrap method on a 1% random sample and present these results in Table IA.5, column 5.

Table IA.6 of theInternet Appendixshows that our results are robust if we conduct the analysis at the individual–bank level as opposed to our baseline models which are estimated at the credit card level. Column 1 shows results with our card-level data aggregated to the individual-bank level by summing up the total credit limits. Columns 2 and 3 report individual-bank level results by retaining the most used card among the multiple cards that an individual has with a particular bank. The most-used card is classified based on the highest utilization ratio in column 2, and the highest balance in column 3. Column 4 excludes individuals with multiple cards at any single bank, which reduces our sample by 8%. The results in Columns 1–4 are similar to our baseline results, thus indicating that multiple cards from the same bank are unlikely to drive our results.

Despite the similarity of the individual-bank-level results, we prefer our baseline analysis at the credit card level because the higher level of disaggregation allows us to flexibly control for credit-card-level variables that are related to credit demand, such as credit card age and card-level utilization.

4.3 Heterogeneity in the pass-through of the funding shock

Next, we examine whether banks transmit their funding shocks differentially across consumers. We focus on two main questions for our heterogeneity analysis. First, how do banks choose their limit cuts across different types of consumers? And second, given the limit cuts, how do these limit cuts change the total borrowing capacities on credit cards across consumers?

To address the first question, we draw on the general intuition from the cost pass-through literature in product markets, andAgarwal et al. (2017)specifically in the context of credit cards. A standard result in the cost pass-through literature is that the cost pass-through depends on the relative slopes of the marginal revenue and marginal cost curves. The pass-through is greater when the marginal revenue and marginal cost curves of the banks areflatter, that is, when the slope of the bank’s marginal profit curve is flatter. Figure IA.5 and Section E in theInternet Appendixvisually demonstrate this result and also derive it formally using a simple model of credit limits for a profit-maximizing bank.

Banking theory suggests that the slope of the marginal profit curve is related to information frictions, such as adverse selection and moral hazard (Stiglitz and Weiss 1981;Ausubel 1999;Agarwal, Chomsisengphet, and Liu 2010;Agarwal et al. 2017). A bank’s marginal profits drop more (i.e., steeper curve) when the marginal borrowing due to an increase in credit limits leads to a greater increase in defaults either because it changes the composition of marginal borrowers (adverse selection à laStiglitz and Weiss 1981) or because greater borrowing causally increases defaults (moral hazard). Using credit card revenue and cost data,Agarwal et al. (2017)empirically show that the marginal profit curves of the lower credit score borrowers are steeper, which is consistent with the greater information frictions associated with lower credit score borrowers.15

Thus, both the theoretical and empirical literature suggests a smaller dollar credit limit cut for borrowers with greater information frictions since their marginal profit curves are steeper. Similarly, borrowers with lower information frictions should experience greater dollar credit limit cuts since their marginal profit curves are flatter.

InTable 5, we explore the heterogeneity of the bank’s funding shock pass-through across consumers based on their credit card utilization ratios and credit scores to the extent that lower credit scores and higher credit utilization proxy for greater information frictions in the credit market.

In terms of their preshock credit limit levels, the point estimates inTable 5andFigure 4reveal an opposite pattern. Panel A ofFigure 4shows that the credit limit cut relative to the borrower’s average preshock credit limit monotonically increases with the borrower’s credit utilization, with an especially sharp increase for borrowers with utilization ratios greater than 90%. Similarly, consumers with credit scores less than 660 experience greater limit cuts as a proportion of their preshock credit limit relative to borrowers with credit scores greater than 660. Overall, these results suggest that the transmission of the funding shock results in a greater reduction of the borrowing capacities of individuals with lower credit scores and higher credit utilization ratios.
article.5 Individual-Level Results: Who Can Hedg:
So far, we have provided evidence for how negative bank liquidity shocks transmit through credit card limits. In this section, we test whether the bank liquidity shocks that are transmitted through credit card limits have an overall effect on consumers’ borrowing capacity and borrowing through credit cards at the individual level. Further, we also examine whether the impact of the transmitted bank liquidity shock is persistent over time or temporary.

5.1 Effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card limits

We first examine whether the transmitted bank liquidity shock affects consumers’ total credit card limits, that is, the overall borrowing capacity of consumers through credit cards. Bank liquidity shocks transmitted through credit limits may not affect consumers’ borrowing capacity if consumers can easily open new credit cards or request higher credit limits from the unaffected credit card lenders. However, if consumers are constrained and face high costs of substituting across credit card lenders, then the transmitted bank liquidity shocks can reduce consumers’ total credit card limits and thus reduce the ability of consumers to borrow on their credit cards.

Our data allow us to test whether the transmitted bank liquidity shock affects the overall borrowing capacity of consumers through credit cards because we observe credit limits onallthe credit cards of a consumer both in the pre- and post-shock periods. We measure a given consumer’s overall borrowing capacity through credit cards by summing the credit limits across all the credit cards of the consumer. We then compute the dollar-change in total credit card limits from the preshock period to the post-shock period to measure the consumer’s change in borrowing capacity through credit cards. Importantly, this measure includes credit cards issued by issuers that do not finance themselves through wholesale funding. As a result, our measure captures cases in which consumers are able to switch to credit card issuers that were unaffected by the short-term wholesale funding shock. This individual-level analysis utilizes the near universe of U.S. credit cards which consists of approximately 500 million credit cards issued to approximately 134 million consumers.

Next, we construct a weighted average exposure measure at the consumer level calledWeighted exposure, which measures the exposure of a consumer to the funding shock through their credit limits.Weighted exposureis constructed by weighting the bank’s exposure variable (i.e., the ratio of short-term wholesale funding to liabilities) associated with a credit card by its credit limit as a proportion of the consumer’s total credit limit. For the credit-card-issuing banks that are not among the 18 banks in our credit-card-level analysis, we assume that their exposure to the short-term wholesale funding market (and thus their exposure to the liquidity shock) is zero. By doing so, we could misclassify some banks as unexposed banks when in fact they experienced a liquidity shock due to the contraction of short-term wholesale funding. As a result, some individuals would be misclassified as unexposed or low-exposure individuals when they are actually high-exposure individuals. However, such a misclassification is likely to underestimate any effect on the change in overall credit card limits.17We implement the following specification for our individual-level analysis using theWeighted exposuremeasure:

α β η ε $ Δ CCLimi t i = α + β Weighted exposur e i + f ( X i ) + η z + ε i , (2)

whereiindexes individuals and $⁠ΔCCLimitiis the dollar-change in total credit card limits aggregated across all credit cards for individualifrom the preshock period to the post-shock period. The variableηzrepresents five digit ZIP code fixed effects, which allow us to compare the changes in credit card limits for consumerswithinthe same ZIP code while controlling for any common shocks at the ZIP code level (e.g., unemployment, house price changes) that can affect a consumer’s total credit demand. The vectorf(Xi)consists of individual-level control variables measured in the preshock period and include credit score, monthly income, debt-to-income ratio, credit card utilization, and credit card and mortgage balances.

Table 6presents results for the impact of the short-term wholesale funding shock on total credit card limits after estimating the specification inEquation (2). Column 1 shows that a one-standard-deviation increase in the weighted exposure measure leads to a $934 reduction in a consumer’s total credit limit, which is a 3.3% reduction in their total borrowing capacity through credit cards relative to their preshock levels. This nonzero coefficient indicates that on average, consumers were unable to offset the reduction in their borrowing capacity resulting from the transmitted funding shock. Columns 2–6 examine the extent to which different types of borrowers are able to hedge against the pass-through of bank liquidity shocks. We find that the credit card borrowing capacities of the very high-utilization ratio and nonprime individuals reduce by 5.7% and 4%, respectively.

5.2 Effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card balances

Thus far, the results show how negative liquidity shocks to banks are transmitted through credit card limits and the types of consumers whose borrowing capacities are more adversely affected. However, a reduction in the borrowing capacities of consumers may not matter if consumers have adequate liquidity buffers in terms of their unused credit limits. Thus, in this section, we test whether the transmitted bank liquidity shocks through credit limits have an overall impact on credit card borrowing.

We regress the dollar change in credit card balances onWeighted exposureusing a specification similar toEquation (2).19Table 7, column 1, shows that a one-standard-deviation increase in the weighted exposure measure leads to an $83 reduction in a consumer’s total credit card balance, which is a 1.6% reduction relative to their preshock total credit card balance. Columns 2–6 show that the impact of the short-term wholesale funding shock on credit card balances monotonically increases with the credit constraints of the individual. For instance, the balance reduction is $352 for very high-utilization ratio and $326 for nonprime individuals. The difference in point estimates between the high- and low-constrained groups is –$354 (t-stat = –4.82) based on credit utilization and –$324 (t-stat = –4.67) based on credit score. Our dollar estimates for credit-constrained consumers are consistent withGross, Notowidigdo, and Wang (2020). They estimate the marginal propensity to borrow (MPB) for a $1 credit limit increase after bankruptcy flag removals for lower-credit score individuals over the business cycle. They estimate an MPB of 45 cents between 2007 and 2009, which is comparable to our estimate of 53 cents (i.e.,326612⁠) based on column 5 ofTable 6and column 5 ofTable 7.

We follow the recent literature and interpret our results on credit card balance changes as the change in credit card borrowing (Keys, Tobacman, and Wang 2017;Barrot et al. 2022;Hundtofte, Olafsson, and Pagel 2019;Braxton, Herkenhoff, and Phillips 2020). We offer further evidence consistent with these motives in Section 5.5.

5.3 Other aggregate effects of the funding shock

In this section, we first attempt to quantify the aggregate effect of the short-term wholesale funding shock through the credit card channel using some simple back-of-the-envelope computation. Then we also attempt to test whether the short-term wholesale funding shock was passed through credit card interest rates.

To quantify the aggregate impact, we use the individual-level estimates fromTables 6and7as they capture the funding shock’s effect at the individual level after aggregating changes in limits and balances across all the cards issued to an individual. Next, we find that a one-standard-deviation increase in theWeighted exposuremeasure is associated with a 3.2% drop in short-term wholesale funding as a fraction of total liabilities from July 2008 to January 2010.22The aggregate drop in short-term wholesale funding over the same period for credit-card-issuing banks is 8.2% as a fraction of total liabilities. This drop in wholesale funding corresponds to a 2.5 (i.e.,8.23.2⁠) standard deviation change in theWeighted exposuremeasure. Based on the full sample (i.e., column 1) estimates inTables 6and7for the 133.5 million consumers, this corresponds to a $311 billion (⁠2.5×934×133.5⁠) reduction in limits and a $27 billion (⁠2.5×83×133.5⁠) reduction in balances due to the sudden drop in short-term wholesale funding for credit-card-issuing banks. Based on the aggregate $1.3 trillion drop in limits and $150 billion drop in balances from July 2008 to January 2010 (see Figure IA.4), we estimate the wholesale funding shock accounted for 24% (i.e.,3111,300⁠) of the aggregate drop in limits and 18% (i.e.,27150⁠) of the aggregate drop in balances.

Next, we also attempt to test whether the funding shock is passed through interest rates. Since we do not observe card-level interest rates in our data, we obtain RateWatch data which provides interest rates at the bank-branch level. However, because of its limited coverage, we are able to obtain interest rate data for only 14 of the 18 banks in our sample. We conduct the analysis using the RateWatch data at the bank-ZIP level and do not find any evidence for the pass-through of the funding shock through credit card interest rates. However, when we conduct a similar aggregated analysis using credit card limits, we continue to find evidence for the pass-through of the funding shock through credit card limits. These results are presented in Table IA.8 and described in detail in Section F of theInternet Appendix.

Our result on the lack of the pass-through of the funding shock through credit prices seems consistent with prior papers that argue that credit card interest rates tend to be relatively sticky and less sensitive to changes (Ausubel 1991;Calem and Mester 1995;Agarwal et al. 2017;Gross, Notowidigdo, and Wang 2020). These papers offer various explanations for the insensitivity of interest rates over time, some of which include adverse selection effects related to higher interest rates (à laStiglitz and Weiss 1981) and anticompetitive behavior across credit card lenders.

5.4 Long-run effect of the funding shock through the credit card channel

First, to understand the long-run effect of the wholesale funding shock on bank credit supply,Figure 7plots the credit card limits extended by the high-exposure (above-median) and low-exposure (below-median) banks relative to the January 2008 archive (i.e., just before the funding shock) over a longer horizon. The credit limits for each group are computed by aggregating credit limits extended onallcredit cards including any newly opened credit cards in the post-shock period that were offered to new consumers. The figure shows that, relative to preshock levels, the aggregate credit limits extended by the high-exposure banks reduced more than the low-exposure banks in the first 3 years after the short-term wholesale funding liquidity shock. Importantly, this also shows that the banks did not simply substitute away by reducing credit from the borrowers in our sample and offering it to new borrowers in the post-shock period. After the first 3 years, the aggregate credit limits extended by the high-exposure banks start to recover toward their preshock levels.23

5.5 Potential channel

In this section, we argue that our results are largely consistent with precautionary saving motives driving the borrowing response of individuals affected by the funding shock. Theoretical models incorporating credit constraints and income uncertainty in incomplete markets suggest that consumers have incentives to accumulate liquidity buffers in order to guard against adverse income fluctuations and the possibility of binding liquidity constraints in the future (Deaton 1991;Carroll 1997). These liquidity buffers, which include a consumer’s liquid savings and (unused) credit limits, serve as insurance in bad states. Intuitively, these models suggest that a reduction in credit limits should reduce the consumer’s liquidity buffer and thus strengthen the consumer’s precautionary saving motive. Importantly, consumers who are especially close to their limits delever by reducing borrowing on credit cards to build credit buffers.

Figure 11tests this idea by plotting the long-run credit card balance dynamics across individuals sorted into 10 nonoverlapping equal-width credit utilization bins based on their preshock credit utilization (Aydin 2022). This plot allows us to examine the credit card balance dynamics in both the short-run and long-run, and across consumers facing different intensities of credit constraints. Panel A plots credit balances divided by preshock credit limits in 2008 (event year 0), that is,Balancet/Limit0⁠. Panel B plots the credit utilization ratio, that is,Balancet/Limitt⁠. The dynamics are plotted separately for high-exposure and low-exposure individuals who are categorized based on their medianWeighted exposuremeasure.

Figure 11shows that high-exposure individuals who are closer to their credit limits delever by reducing their credit card balance and their credit utilization ratio, thereby seeking to build credit buffers. In contrast, low-exposure individuals who are closer to their credit limits more or less continued to borrow on their credit cards while also reducing their credit utilization ratio. Thus, both types of constrained individuals reduce their credit utilization ratios and build credit buffers when they are close to their credit limits. However, since the high-exposure individuals experience credit limit cuts due to the transmitted funding shock, they are compelled to build credit buffers by deleveraging and reducing their credit card debt.

Further, high-exposure individuals who are far from their credit limit, and thus have significant liquidity buffers, also reduce their rate of credit card borrowing relative to low-exposure individuals. Together, these trends provide support for precautionary saving motives and buffer stock models. Precautionary saving motives in these models broadly arise through two mechanisms. First, if individuals exhibit prudent preferences (i.e., a convex marginal utility of consumption), then a reduction in credit limits, which makes consumption more volatile, can lead to the precautionary saving behavior even in the absence of credit constraints (Kimbal 1990).24Second, such precautionary saving behavior can also arise under credit constraints as individuals try to avoid zero consumption, even if these individuals do not have prudent preferences.

5.6 Why is the effect of the funding shock persistent?

The results in the previous subsection show that the short-term wholesale funding shock has a persistent effect on consumers’ credit card limits. One possible explanation for this result is that the transmitted funding shock itself weakens a consumer’s fundamentals, thereby limiting her access to future credit. Alternatively, another possibility is that certain credit market frictions for consumers could be binding for a very long time. We investigate these possibilities to better understand what drives the persistent effect of the funding shock.

The transmitted bank funding shock can lead to other adverse knock-on effects which can weaken the affected consumers’ fundamentals and limit their access to credit during the recovery period. For instance, in order to cope with their lower credit card borrowing capacities resulting from the transmitted bank funding shock, if the affected consumers resort to borrowing from more expensive sources of credit, then it might lower their ability to repay debt and weaken consumer fundamentals, such as credit scores. We examine this knock-on effects channel by regressing the change in consumers’ credit scores from the preshock period to a given post-shock period on the weighted exposure measure and other controls using the same specification asEquation (3).

Figure 12, panel A plots the coefficients for the weighted exposure measure along with their confidence intervals over time. The figure indicates that the credit scores of consumers do not deteriorate over time and are practically unaffected by the transmitted funding shock. Panel B analyzes an alternate measure of consumer fundamentals, namely, the consumer’s monthly income as modeled by the credit bureau.25Financial stress induced by the transmitted funding shock could affect consumers’ job performance and consequently their income (Bernstein, McQuade, and Townsend 2021;Maturana and Nickerson 2020). The figure in panel B suggests that the transmitted funding shock did not affect consumers’ incomes in any economically significant manner. For instance, the monthly income changes of affected consumers relative to the unaffected consumers are within $30.

Overall,Figure 12suggests that the deterioration of consumers’ credit fundamentals or negative shocks to consumers’ permanent incomes are unlikely to explain the persistence of the transmitted bank funding shock. The absence of these channels might suggest that certain credit market frictions could be binding for consumers for a long time. For instance, there might be a path dependence in credit limits when lenders make future lending decisions, which results in the inability of the affected consumers to access credit during the recovery period. Such a path-dependence has been documented in the credit market where lenders seem to anchor to historical credit terms when offering new loans to firms (Dougal et al. 2015).

We test the path-dependence hypothesis using a specification similar toLemmon, Roberts, and Zender (2008), who document a path-dependence in firm leverage. We estimate the following cross-sectional regression specification for each post-shock year:

Thus, to the extent that credit limits are primarily determined by contemporaneous observable borrower fundamentals, historical credit limits should not influence future credit limits, andβshould equal zero.

Figure 13plotsβestimated over time for both the prime and nonprime consumers. First, the estimatedβis positive and statistically significant even until 10 years after the funding shock, which suggests a strong path dependence in credit card limits. For instance, the estimates suggest that a $1 lower credit limit immediately after the shock corresponds to a 40-cent lower credit limit 10 years after the shock. Second, these estimates are similar for both the prime and nonprime consumers, which suggests that the factors causing the path dependence in credit card limits may not be related to the credit quality of consumers. As a possibility, such factors could be related to institutional frictions on the lenders’ side, where lenders tend to raise credit limits governed by rules of thumb, as documented inGross and Souleles (2002).

5.7 Are consumers able to access other credit sources?

Finally, we also test whether consumers affected by the funding shock are able to access other credit sources to offset the reduction in their credit card limits. For instance, consumers can obtain personal installment loans to cope with the reduction in their borrowing capacity through credit cards. Using the credit bureau data, we calculate individual-level debt balances aggregated across all of a consumer’s nonmortgage debt accounts, thus allowing us to test this hypothesis.

Table IA.10 of theInternet Appendixpresents the regression results of the change in individual-level debt balances from the pre- to post-shock period on the weighted exposure measure. These individual-level debt balances also include credit card balances. Therefore, if the consumers who were adversely affected by their bank’s funding shock are able to access other sources of credit (e.g., personal installment loans), then the coefficient for the weighted exposure measure should be statistically and economically insignificant.

The results in Table IA.10 show that the credit-constrained consumers who were more adversely affected by the pass-through of the bank funding shock were unable to access other sources of credit to mitigate the impact of the transmitted bank funding shock through their credit cards. This can be seen from the point estimates in Table IA.10, which are higher than the dollar estimates of the weighted exposure’s effect on credit card balances presented inTable 7. However, a limitation of these results is that we do not observe credit sources that are not reported to the credit bureau, such as payday loans, which can be a source of credit for credit-constrained consumers.
article.6 Conclusion:
In this paper, we show that adverse liquidity shocks to banks can transmit through the credit card channel to consumers with significant distributional consequences. Specifically, we use micro data on credit limits and credit balances for the near universe of U.S. consumer credit cards and show how the dry-up of the short-term wholesale funding market for banks affects their consumers.

We document significant heterogeneity in how banks transmit their liquidity shocks to consumers through credit card limits. In general, credit-constrained consumers experience a greater pass-through of their bank’s funding shock. As credit-constrained consumers generally face greater credit market frictions, they are unable to hedge away from the transmitted bank liquidity shocks and reduce their credit card borrowing. Moreover, we show that the transmitted bank funding shock has a persistent effect on these consumers’ credit card borrowing. Consequently, our results showwhobears the costs of fragile bank funding structures and for how long.

Our results also contribute to the debate on post-crisis regulatory reform on banks’ funding structures that have become heavily reliant on wholesale funding. Our results provide estimates of how adverse shocks to banks’ wholesale funding transmit through the credit card lending channel across different consumer groups based on their credit scores and credit utilization. Consequently, our results enrich the debate on the distributional effects of banks’ funding fragility on consumers.
